data.title: Nasdaq 100 futures drop after another AI trade stumble
data.description:
Stock index futures pointed to a mixed open Friday as AI momentum hit another speed bump.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) slipped -0.11%, while Nasdaq futures (US100:IND) declined -0.47%. Dow futures (INDU) bucked the trend, rising +0.22%.

"The swing in AI sentiment continued with Broadcom’s (AVGO) earnings after the US close," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. "Following a +78% advance YTD, the chipmaker now has a larger market cap than Meta and Tesla, playing a growing role in the tech market narrative." "The stock initially advanced +4% after-hours after unveiling stronger-than-expected revenue guidance for the current quarter ($19.1bn vs $18.5bn est.). But it then turned sharply lower as management held off on giving an AI revenue forecast for the year."

Treasury yields showed modest movement in early trading. The 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) edged lower by -0.008 to 3.54%, while the 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) inched up 0.01 to 4.17%. The 30-Year Treasury yield (US30Y) added 0.02 to 4.82%, reflecting ongoing investor reassessment of economic conditions.

Top gainers in premarket trading included lululemon athletica (LULU) +9.64%, Newmont Corporation (NEM) +2.20%, and Invitation Homes (INVH) +2.00%. Notable decliners were led by Broadcom (AVGO) -4.89%, Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) -3.97%, and Fastenal Company (FAST) -2.95%.

data.title: Broadcom stock falls as management warns of margin pressures
data.description:
Shares of Broadcom (AVGO) dropped around 5% premarket on Friday after its fourth-quarter earnings results and amid a broader selloff in the AI and tech space.

The chipmaker reported better-than-expected results for Q4, buoyed by a continued AI spending boom, leading to a rise in share prices in extended trading on Thursday, but premarket trading has reversed the trend, with investors appearing cautious of AI returns.

In the post-earnings call, CFO Kirsten Spears noted margin pressures in the near term. “We expect Q1 consolidated gross margin to be down approximately 100 basis points sequentially, primarily reflecting a higher mix of AI revenue. As a reminder, consolidated gross margins through the year will be impacted by the revenue mix of Infrastructure Software and Semiconductors and also product mix within Semiconductors,” the executive said.

Meanwhile, CEO Hock Tan said the company has a $73 billion backlog in AI product orders scheduled for delivery over the next six quarters—a figure that left some investors underwhelmed. Tan later clarified that this amount represents a “minimum” level of expected shipments.

Tan said, “We do expect much more as more orders come in for shipments within that next six quarters. “So our lead time, depending on the particular product it is, can be anywhere from six months to a year.”

Broadcom’s (AVGO) premarket dip also seems to be mirroring a larger decline in the AI space amid concerns of an AI bubble. U.S. AI names were trading in red in Friday premarket for the third day. Oracle (ORCL) is down 0.9%, as are Nvidia (NVDA) -0.8%, Micron (MU) -1.7% and CoreWeave (CRWV) -1.2%.

data.title: Broadcom stocks fall as management warns of margin pressures
data.description:
Shares of Broadcom (AVGO) dropped around 5% premarket on Friday after its fourth-quarter earnings results and amid a broader selloff in the AI and tech space.

The chipmaker reported better-than-expected results for Q4 and guidance, buoyed by a continued AI spending boom, leading to a rise in share prices in extended trading on Thursday, but premarket trading has reversed the trend, with investors appearing cautious of AI returns.

In the post-earnings call, CFO Kirsten Spears noted margin pressures in the near term.

“We expect Q1 consolidated gross margin to be down approximately 100 basis points sequentially, primarily reflecting a higher mix of AI revenue. As a reminder, consolidated gross margins through the year will be impacted by the revenue mix of Infrastructure Software and Semiconductors and also product mix within Semiconductors,” the executive said.

Meanwhile, CEO Hock Tan said the company has a $73 billion backlog in AI product orders scheduled for delivery over the next six quarters—a figure that left some investors underwhelmed. Tan later clarified that this amount represents a “minimum” level of expected shipments.

Tan said “We do expect much more as more orders come in for shipments within that next six quarters. “So our lead time, depending on the particular product it is, can be anywhere from six months to a year.”

Broadcom’s (AVGO) premarket dip also seems to be mirroring a larger decline in the AI space amid concerns of an AI bubble. U.S. AI names were trading in red in Friday premarket for the third day. Oracle (ORCL) is down 0.9%, as are Nvidia (NVDA) -0.8%, Micron (MU) -1.7% and CoreWeave (CRWV) -1.2%.

data.title: NATO chief: We are Russia's next target
data.description:
"We are Russia's next target, and we are already in harm's way," NATO chief Mark Rutte warned as he called on allies to ramp up defense spending and production.

"Russia has brought war back to Europe, and we must be prepared for the scale of war our grandparents or great-grandparents endured," Rutte said during a speech in Berlin.

While he welcomed NATO members' decision to increase annual military spending to 5% of GDP by 2035, he said this was not the time for self-congratulation.

"I fear that too many are quietly complacent, and too many don't feel the urgency, too many believe that time is on our side. It is not, the time for action is now," he warned.

Rutte noted that NATO's defenses can hold for now, "but with its economy dedicated to war, Russia could be ready to use military force against NATO within five years."

He also pointed to China being "Russia's lifeline" in the war on Ukraine, saying Chinese technology is often used in the weapons that kill Ukrainians.

"… and let's not forget that Russia also relies on North Korea and Iran in its fight against freedom, with ammunition and military equipment," Rutte added.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AVGO, LULU, and more
data.description:
Stock futures hovered around the flatline on Friday morning, signaling a muted open at the end of a week dominated by the Federal Reserve’s latest 25-basis-point rate cut and its cautious guidance on the policy path for 2026.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • lululemon athletica (LULU) +9% - Shares surged after the company raised its full-year forecast, posted a strong Q3, and boosted its stock buyback program by $1B. For FY2025, Lululemon now expects EPS of $12.92–$13.02, with the $12.97 midpoint topping the $12.89 consensus and up from the prior $12.77–$12.97 range. Revenue is projected at $10.96B–$11.05B, with the $11.01B midpoint exceeding the $10.96B consensus and above the earlier $10.85B–$11B outlook. The company also announced it has hired an executive search firm to identify its next CEO, as Calvin McDonald will step down at the end of January, while board chair Marti Morfitt immediately assumes an expanded role as executive chair.
  • InterContinental Hotels (IHG) +4% - Shares saw the biggest gain in four months after Jefferies upgraded the stock to Buy from Hold and lifted its price target to 11,400p from 8,700p. The analysts said hotels remain one of the more attractive pockets of opportunity amid increasingly limited growth across the broader consumer sector.

Biggest stock losers

  • Broadcom (AVGO) -5% - Shares dipped even as the company posted FQ4 results and guidance that exceeded Wall Street’s expectations, fueled by ongoing AI spending. Revenue rose 28% Y/Y to a record $18B, driven by a 74% jump in AI semiconductor sales. CEO Hock Tan said momentum will continue into Q1, with AI chip revenue expected to double Y/Y to $8.2B, supported by demand for custom AI accelerators and Ethernet AI switches. Broadcom guided for Q1 revenue of ~$19.1B and adjusted EBITDA of 67%, above the $18.38B consensus. The company also raised its quarterly dividend by 10% to $0.65 per share, payable Dec. 31.

data.title: Destination XL outlines $25M synergy target and merger-driven transformation with FullBeauty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Destination XL Group, Inc. (DXLG) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Harvey Kanter, President, CEO & Director, highlighted a transformative merger, stating, "Today marks a pivotal step in redefining inclusive apparel as DXL and FullBeauty join forces to create a retailer that sets a new standard for choice, quality and customer experience." He emphasized the strategic fit of the merger, focusing on complementary missions targeting underserved markets and significant growth opportunities.
  • Kanter detailed the merger as a "merger of equals," creating a "scaled category-defining retailer for inclusive apparel" with an aim to leverage combined data science, proprietary fit technology, and digital scale. He noted, "By building on our combined strengths, we will meet the opportunity by creating a powerful engine for innovation, combining data science, digital scale, proprietary fit technology and differentiated store expertise."
  • James Fogarty, Chief Executive Officer of FullBeauty and incoming CEO of the combined company, explained, "For DXL shareholders, this means owning a larger, more diversified company with higher EBITDA and stronger value creation prospects than DXL on a stand-alone basis."
  • Kanter explained the financial structure, stating, "The transaction is 100% stock for stock with DXL shareholders owning 45% and FullBeauty shareholders owning 55% of the combined company." He also outlined a "committed subscription of $92 million through the sale of common stock," resulting in an expected term loan of $172 million with maturity in August 2029.
  • Peter Stratton, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, reported, "Net sales for the third quarter were $101.9 million as compared to $107.5 million in the third quarter of last year...the quarterly comp was an improvement from negative 9.3% in the first half of the year."
  • Stratton added, "EBITDA for the quarter came in at a loss of $2 million as compared to earnings of $1 million for the third quarter of last year."

Outlook

  • The transaction is expected to close in the first half of fiscal 2026, subject to customary closing conditions and shareholder approval. Kanter stated, "We intend to begin capturing these synergies promptly after the closing of the transaction with a significant portion to be actioned within the first 12 months."
  • The combination is expected to generate $25 million in run rate annual cost synergies by 2027.
  • Fogarty projected, "For the last 12 months ending October 2025, DXL and FullBeauty generated approximately $1.2 billion in combined net sales. Assuming no pro forma adjustments, adjusted EBITDA was approximately $45 million. With the $25 million in expected annual run rate cost synergies, adjusted EBITDA for the LTM would have been approximately $70 million."

Financial Results

  • Stratton reported, "Net sales for the third quarter were $101.9 million as compared to $107.5 million in the third quarter of last year. The decrease in net sales was primarily due to a decrease in comparable sales of 7.4%, partially offset by an increase in noncomparable sales from new stores."
  • Gross margin rate, inclusive of occupancy costs, was 42.7% compared to 45.1% the previous year. Stratton indicated, "Tariffs impacted our third quarter margins by approximately 60 basis points, and we expect the impact on our fiscal year 2025 margin to be approximately $2 million."
  • SG&A expense as a percentage of sales increased to 44.7% compared to 44.1% a year earlier. EBITDA for the quarter was a loss of $2 million versus earnings of $1 million last year.
  • The company finished the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $27 million and excess availability of $73.6 million under its revolving credit facility, with no outstanding debt in either period.

Q&A

  • Jeremy Hamblin, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: Asked for details on the expected capital structure post-closing, the expected cash balance, and CapEx plans. Stratton answered, "The total debt that we're expecting upon closing is the $172 million...there's going to be a lot more that will be coming soon, but that's just a quick start." Fogarty added, "the maturity is out to August of 2029 on the term loan, and it's LIBOR plus 750."
  • Hamblin also inquired about sales trends and brand strategy for FullBeauty. Fogarty responded, "All of our brands serve a purpose. If you look at...the new mall brands service millennial, younger Gen X demo. And then the classic mall brands have historically serviced the sort of older Gen X and into young boomers demo."
  • Hamblin raised questions about promotional strategy alignment between DXL and FullBeauty. Stratton responded, "We're targeting $25 million of run rate cost synergies. We're going to start capturing those we think, pretty quickly after closing."
  • Michael Baker, D.A. Davidson & Co.: Asked about stock issuance and capital structure. Stratton clarified, "It's going to be 55% to FullBeauty and 45% to DXL will be the pro forma ownership."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on the transaction's structure, synergy potential, and capital allocation, with repeated congratulations on the merger but pressing for specific financial details and clarity on post-close metrics.
  • Management expressed confidence throughout, with Kanter calling the merger a "pivotal step" and Fogarty emphasizing the "exciting day for both DXL and FullBeauty." Stratton and Fogarty offered detailed, though sometimes limited, responses, often deferring specifics to upcoming proxy statements.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism about standalone prospects to strong confidence and enthusiasm regarding the merger and its anticipated synergies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marks a strategic shift, moving from a focus on private brand penetration and cost control to announcing a transformative merger with FullBeauty, targeting $25 million in annual cost synergies.
  • Key metrics such as net sales and EBITDA declined year-over-year and sequentially, but there was a noted improvement in comparable sales versus the first half of the year.
  • Previous quarter discussions centered on internal initiatives and market pressures, while the current quarter's focus is on transaction benefits, integration synergies, and establishing a category-defining inclusive apparel retailer.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from operational margin and promotional discipline to capital structure, debt, and integration plans for the combined entity.
  • Management’s confidence increased, supported by the merger’s potential scale and synergy benefits, while analysts remained inquisitive but receptive.

Risks and Concerns

  • Stratton noted, "Tariffs impacted our third quarter margins by approximately 60 basis points, and we expect the impact on our fiscal year 2025 margin to be approximately $2 million."
  • The company acknowledged ongoing consumer caution and discretionary spending softness, with Kanter stating, "We continue to see a shift towards our value-driven private brands as customers remain cautious with their discretionary spending."
  • Integration risk and realization of anticipated synergies were implied, as substantial details were deferred pending further disclosures in proxy materials.

Final Takeaway

Destination XL Group's Q3 2025 call centered on the announced merger with FullBeauty, which is expected to create a category-defining leader in inclusive apparel. Management underscored the $25 million annual synergy target, the enhanced scale for omnichannel growth, and the expanded customer reach. While current financial results reflected ongoing sales challenges and margin pressures, leadership expressed strong confidence in the merger’s transformational potential and its ability to deliver long-term shareholder value through operational efficiencies, commercial synergies, and a diversified brand portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: India gets Ozempic at $24/week as Novo capitalizes on diabetes market growth
data.description:
• Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk (NVO) launched Ozempic in India on Friday, pricing the 0.25 mg dose at $24.35 per week.
  • Novo Nordisk (NVO) received regulatory approval in September to import and sell the drug in the country.
  • Ozempic, a once-weekly injectable approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration in 2017 for type 2 diabetes, has become a global bestseller and is widely used off-label for weight loss due to its appetite-suppressing effects.
  • Novo's Wegovy, which, like Ozempic, is semaglutide, is approved for weight loss.
  • India has the second-highest number of people living with type 2 diabetes after China, alongside rapidly increasing obesity rates. Analysts cited by Reuters estimate the global market could be worth as much as $150 billion annually by the end of the decade.

data.title: Foxconn to build new $510 million headquarters in southern Taiwan
data.description:
Foxconn (FXCOF) plans a major $510 million investment in its new Kaohsiung headquarters in southern Taiwan. The project underscores the company's expanding focus on AI, EVs, and smart city technologies.

The contract electronics maker will use the funds for a mixed-use commercial and office building plus a residential tower. Construction is expected to start in 2027 and targets completion by 2033.

The headquarters will centralize Foxconn's southern Taiwan operations, hosting teams for smart cities, software R&D, battery cells, EV tech, and AI applications. It builds on prior Kaohsiung commitments, with city investments reaching $801.8 million over three years.

data.title: Rio Tinto’s Tomago smelter gets government support in rescue plan
data.description:
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese on Friday announced a rescue effort to keep open the country's largest aluminium smelter, the struggling Tomago facility, majority-owned by Rio Tinto (RIO).

The development comes after the company warned in October that Tomago Aluminium, the employer of more than 1,000 full-time staff and 200 contractors, might be forced to shut down after failing to secure new, affordable energy supplies.

Aluminium was "increasingly a vital product", Albanese said, making it important to keep Tomago open.

"If Australia doesn't produce aluminium, then the knock-on effect in other industries is significant," he said at the smelter, located north of Sydney in the state of New South Wales (NSW), Reuters reported.

"We are working with the company, we are working with the New South Wales government to make sure there are a long-term energy solutions to go forward beyond 2028."

The deal, which has yet to be finalised, would involve securing a long-term, fixed-price energy supply for the smelter, the government said.

data.title: RH targets 9%–9.2% revenue growth and $250M–$300M free cash flow for fiscal 2025 while innovating amid challenging housing market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RH (RH) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Gary Friedman, Chairman & CEO, reported "industry-leading growth with revenue increasing 9% in the third quarter and up 18% on a 2-year basis, demonstrating the disruptive nature of our brand despite the worst housing market in almost 50 years and the polarizing impact of tariffs." He noted an adjusted operating margin of 11.6%, below the 12.5% midpoint of guidance, citing "higher-than-forecasted tariff expense on prior period special order and back order sales delivered in the quarter and higher-than-expected tariffs opening expenses." Adjusted EBITDA was 17.6% and free cash flow reached $83 million in Q3, with year-to-date free cash flow at $198 million, maintaining the target of $250 million to $300 million for the year.
  • The company continues to reduce excess inventory, estimated at $300 million, with inventory down 11% versus last year and down $82 million versus the second quarter. Net debt at quarter-end was $2.427 billion, down $85 million from Q2.
  • RH highlighted ongoing market share gains, noting "share gains on a 2-year basis range from a low of 12 points to a high of 28 points."
  • Friedman described the launch of RH Paris as "what might be the most beautiful and talked about retail experience in the world," and stated, "we are developing a global hospitality business that generates significant brand awareness, traffic and cash flow."
  • The CEO emphasized the company’s strategy to innovate and invest through uncertain times, referencing a forthcoming "prolific product transformation" and a new concept launching in spring next year, intended to "reaccelerate our growth and create another step change in our business."
  • Jack Preston, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "The tariff piece, I don't -- we didn't materially change the impact from a basis point perspective. Obviously, that's just representative of cost alone."

Outlook

  • RH provided updated financial guidance for Q4: revenue growth of 7% to 8%, adjusted operating margin of 12.5% to 13.5%, and adjusted EBITDA margin of 18.7% to 19.6%. For fiscal year 2025, the outlook is revenue growth of 9% to 9.2%, adjusted operating margin of 11.6% to 11.9%, adjusted EBITDA margin of 17.6% to 18%, and free cash flow of $250 million to $300 million.
  • The outlook reflects a negative operating margin impact from international expansion investments and tariffs, with specific mention of "an approximately negative 210 basis point operating margin impact from investments to start-up cost to support our international expansion and a 90 basis point impact from tariffs net of mitigation."
  • Management continues to highlight the unpredictability of the current environment, referencing "the worst housing market in almost 50 years" and ongoing tariff disruptions.

Financial Results

  • Q3 revenue increased 9%. Adjusted operating margin was 11.6%; adjusted EBITDA margin stood at 17.6%. Free cash flow for Q3 was $83 million, and net debt fell to $2.427 billion. Inventory declined by $82 million compared to Q2.
  • The company’s real estate assets are believed to have an estimated equity value of approximately $500 million, which management plans to monetize opportunistically.
  • RH is making progress on reducing excess inventory, with inventory down 11% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about RH Paris's demand and its influence on expectations for RH Milan and RH London. Gary Friedman described Paris as "really quite different" and noted a strong ramp in core business, with "the average per week is 62% higher than the first 8 weeks" following opening. He outlined lessons learned and how these are informing upcoming launches in Milan and London.
  • Maksim Rakhlenko, TD Cowen: Inquired about recent price increases and customer response. Friedman responded, "We've taken a lot of price increases this year. We've had a lot of movement in tariffs... I think so far, as long as it's fair to everyone, I think that there are -- I think that there's some businesses that might be kind of violating the rules."
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Asked whether RH should slow the pace of initiatives for predictability and profitability. Friedman replied, "I actually like the discipline of being a public company... The thing I've learned and I've observed, I think so many people, they get so focused like a quarterly results that becomes their whole mission as a CEO or a leadership team is like, how do we make the quarter and they do a lot of stupid things to make a quarter that aren't -- we're in building or business model building or anything."
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Asked about furniture demand and free cash flow outlook. Friedman stated, "we expect free cash flow to remain positive. But again, did we expect at any time early in the year that we were going to have all the tariff announcements, such an unpredictable chaotic way... It's a really unusual time."
  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: Requested insights on market share gains from fragmented design showrooms and regional stores. Friedman indicated the majority of share gains are from high-end independents and regional stores, noting, "we believe most of the share came from the higher end and came from like -- it came to the showrooms and they came from the independents, and they came from the regional furniture stores and they came from the Ethan Allen of the world or people like that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism and sought clarity around the impact of tariffs, the pace of initiatives, and margin sustainability. The tone was probing, especially regarding the predictability of results and the sustainability of margin improvements.
  • Management maintained a confident, long-term outlook in prepared remarks, but showed more caution and acknowledgment of uncertainty during the Q&A. Friedman often emphasized innovation during challenges, stating, "necessity is the mother of invention."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted slightly more cautious regarding tariffs and the unpredictability of the market, while analysts' line of questioning became more pointed around risks and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth rate increased slightly from 8.4% in Q2 to 9% in Q3, while adjusted operating margin declined from 15.1% to 11.6%. Adjusted EBITDA margin also decreased from 20.6% in Q2 to 17.6% in Q3.
  • Free cash flow remained robust, with $83 million generated in Q3 versus $81 million in Q2.
  • Management’s guidance for full-year revenue growth narrowed to 9% to 9.2%, compared to 9% to 11% previously, and operating margin guidance was lowered to 11.6%–11.9% from 13%–14%.
  • Management continues to highlight international expansion and product innovation as key strategic focuses, but flagged increased headwinds from tariffs and a more unpredictable operating environment compared to Q2.
  • Analyst focus remained on tariffs, pricing, and predictability of results, with increased scrutiny on margin sustainability and inventory management compared to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged "the worst housing market in almost 50 years" and the "polarizing impact of tariffs" as significant risks, with tariffs causing "significant resourcing, product delays, out of stocks, and driven multiple rounds of price negotiations and increases."
  • Ongoing investments in international expansion are creating a negative impact on near-term operating margins.
  • Management noted the unpredictability of the business environment, with Friedman stating, "Not a time to underestimate risk, tariffs are disrupting supply chains and driving higher prices."
  • Analysts voiced concerns about the ability to offset tariffs with pricing and the sustainability of margin improvement.

Final Takeaway

Management underscores its commitment to long-term value creation by innovating and investing through industry turbulence, pointing to strong revenue growth, market share gains, and progress in inventory reduction despite ongoing macro and tariff headwinds. While operating margins and guidance have been revised downward in the face of persistent uncertainty, RH’s leadership stresses confidence in its global expansion, new product launches, and the unique positioning of its brand and hospitality concepts to drive future growth, even in a challenging operating environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
